name: Fee Simple Absolute
description: The baseline common-law ownership form: broad control, exclusion, transferability, and descent without an internal time limit.

name: Fee Simple Defeasible
description: An ownership form burdened by a condition or executory limitation: still broad, but less secure in time and marketability than fee simple absolute. The power of alienation itself remains unrestricted — the grantee takes subject to the condition.

name: Life Estate
description: A possessory estate measured by one life: strong use and control during the term, but no descendible duration and only narrow transfer of the limited estate.

name: Leasehold
description: A term-based possessory estate that grants exclusive occupation and use but remains bounded by term, covenants, and the landlord’s reversion. Alienability varies widely: commercial leases are often freely assignable, while residential tenancies typically restrict or prohibit assignment.

name: Licence
description: A permission to enter or use property without a proprietary estate: fragile in duration, weak in exclusion, and generally non-transferable.

name: Easement
description: A limited non-possessory right over another parcel, typically durable and descendible, but narrow in use and minimal in exclusion.

name: Restrictive Covenant
description: An enduring negative control over land use: very low possession and use, but stronger exclusionary effect through limits imposed on another owner.

name: Mortgage
description: A security estate or charge whose power is contingent and enforcement-oriented: thin on ordinary use, but meaningful in duration, transfer, and value capture on default.

name: Trust Beneficial Interest
description: The beneficiary’s equitable position under a trust: strong economic entitlement with limited direct possession, exclusion, and transfer relative to legal title.

name: Adverse Possession
description: The inchoate possessory state during a limitation period: factually strong and exclusionary, but without formal title until the period completes. The result of successful adverse possession is typically fee simple. Note: the Land Registration Act 2002 (England/Wales) and Torrens systems (Australia) have significantly curtailed this doctrine for registered land.

name: Profit à Prendre
description: A right to remove value from another’s land, such as minerals or timber: narrow possession, moderate use, strong income capture, and durable appurtenant transmission.

name: Fee Tail (Historical)
description: The historical entailed estate: strong possession, use, and duration tied to lineal descendants, but near-zero alienation by design. Abolished in England (LPA 1925 s.130) and most US states; included here for its importance in illustrating how the estate system constrains the alienation dimension while preserving all others.

name: Future Interest (Remainder / Reversion)
description: A non-possessory interest that becomes possessory upon the natural or stipulated termination of a prior estate. No current control, use, or income, but potentially strong duration and descent. Vested remainders are freely alienable; contingent remainders and executory interests are more restricted.
